references.title: Predicting student academic performance using multi-model heterogeneous ensemble approach
references.journal: Journal of Applied Research in Higher Education

extracted.Features: [Age, Gender, Ethnicity, Postcode location (deprivation level proxy), Campus of study, Mode of study (full-time / part-time), Entry qualification, Disability status, Total login time, Number of resources viewed, Number of module quiz attempts submitted, Number of module forum course views, Number of module forum discussion reads/views, Parent economic status, Work hours, Work pressure, Entry qualification (self-reported context variable), Average study hours, Family support, Course satisfaction, Technology impact, Learning style, Health status, First in university (first-generation student), Adaptation (adjustment to university), University support, Prior course knowledge]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors test different configurations of features: 

Data from SIS alone, LMS alone, Survey Alone and combinations of different data sources.

Banner data includes 8 features, LMS 5 and Survey includes 14 features

Hyperparameter tuning is achieved using 10-Fold CV
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.8295 - SVM - Survey, RMSE: 0.396 - Stacking - All Sources, Precision: 0.7962- Stacking - All Sources, Recall: 0.7586 - Stacking - All Sources, F1-Score: 0.7769 - Stacking - All Sources}
extracted.Comments: This study investigates whether combining multiple data sources and heterogeneous ensemble methods improves the prediction of student academic performance. Using data from 141 students at the University of the West of Scotland, the authors integrate demographic (SRS), behavioral (LMS), and psychological/survey variables. They compare decision tree, neural network, and support vector machine classifiers against a stacking ensemble approach. The hybrid ensemble model that combines all three data sources achieves the highest predictive performance (around 81% accuracy). The authors conclude that multi-source ensemble modeling can support early identification of students at risk of poor performance and potential attrition.
extracted.Limitations:
Unclear operational definition of performance

Temporal ambiguity

Small sample size

Single-institution context

Limited transparency on course scope

No early prediction

annotations.text: Goal of paper is to compare performance of student performance prediction models across different data sources and predictive models.

Authors considered 141 students and, for those, relied on 3 distinct data sources: Student Record System, LMS and survey data. Then, the authors attempt 3 base learners and a stack of different base learners. Ensembles trained with data from multiple data sources outperforms the remaining options. Models trained use accuracy. Also, no mention of a specific moment of prediction.
